content:
Microsoft had a complicated relationship with developers but, this would soon come to an end.

5 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Photo by Mohammad Rahmani on UnsplashOn one hand, its tools powered a huge portion of enterprise software. On the other, many developers associated Microsoft with closed systems, heavy IDEs, and a philosophy that felt increasingly out of step with the open-source world. By the early 2010s, that reputation had become a real problem.
Developers were moving toward lightweight tools, open ecosystems, and cross-platform workflows. Meanwhile, Microsoft was still strongly tied to its traditional development environments. It almost seemed like the company had missed the shift entirely.
But, you guessed it. Something happened and it changed a lot if not everything. Microsoft rebuilt its identity from the inside out and one of the most important symbols of that transformation was Visual Studio Code.
A Changing Developer World
By the time Visual Studio Code was conceived, the software development world had already changed dramatically. Developers no longer wanted heavy, slow tools that took minutes to load. They wanted speed, flexibility, and customization. Many had shifted…
